settings.title: Settings
settings.replayTutorialDesc: See the home screen guide from the start
settings.signOutConfirmMessage: Are you sure you want to sign out?

progress.title: Today's progress
progress.body: See completed and remaining tasks at a glance.
progress.rationale: ADHD brains rely on visualized progress to stay motivated. The large progress ring lets even small wins register.

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.body: Pick one thing to focus on first.
mission.rationale: One thing at a time — multitasking is the heaviest load on an ADHD brain. Narrowing to a single mission lowers the start barrier.

dailyCare.title: Daily care
dailyCare.body: Opens Sleep Garden, ADHD Basics, and Tidy-up screens.
dailyCare.rationale: Sleep, self-understanding, and a tidy environment are the three load-bearing axes of ADHD daily life — gathered here so the flow doesn't break.

planning.title: Planning
planning.body: Daily plan, projects, AI planning, data cleanup screens.
planning.rationale: The act of planning itself is a heavy cognitive load for ADHD. AI assistance and a visual planner lower the start barrier.

thoughts.title: Thoughts & memory
thoughts.rationale: ADHD makes it easy to forget your motivation and the people who matter. A short note about why and who helps next time you get stuck.

references.title: References
references.intro: ADHD-related content in DayStep draws on the following peer-reviewed academic sources.
items: [1] Hvolby, A. (2015). Associations of sleep disturbance with ADHD: implications for treatment. ADHD Attention Deficit and Hyperactivity Disorders, 7(1), 1–18.
[2] Coogan, A. N. & McGowan, N. M. (2017). A systematic review of circadian function, chronotype and chronotherapy in ADHD. ADHD Attention Deficit and Hyperactivity Disorders, 9(3), 129–147.
[3] Volkow, N. D. et al. (2012). Evidence that sleep deprivation downregulates dopamine D2R in ventral striatum in the human brain. Journal of Neuroscience, 32(19), 6711–6717.
[4] Kooij, J. J. S. & Bijlenga, D. (2013). The circadian rhythm in adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: current state of affairs. Expert Review of Neurotherapeutics, 13(10), 1107–1116.

disclaimer.title: Educational use
disclaimer.body: This content is provided for educational and self-management support only. It does not replace professional medical diagnosis or treatment. If difficulties with sleep, attention, or daily functioning persist, consult a psychiatrist or qualified healthcare provider.
